data.title: Iamgold: Marginal But Mighty When Gold Soars (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Since I last covered IAMGOLD (NYSE:IAG) back in April, shares have nearly doubled.

Gold Stalling After Showing Signs Of Renewed Momentum

Gold started last week on a strong note, showing promising momentum on the 1-month chart as it broke through resistance near $4,150 and pushed toward $4,340. But the rally quickly lost steam by Friday. XAU slipped back below that same support-turned-resistance level and is now trading under the 20-day, 50-day, and 200-day moving average yet again—with the 50-day crossing below the 200-day in a classic "death cross."

The 6-month chart shows that XAU is holding just above the 20-day moving average (in purple). If XAU breaks below the 20-day moving average, it is likely to test the 50-day moving average again (in green).

Realised volatility (1-month realised volatility compared to the 1-year average) looks to be fading as well—therefore I expect a period of consolidation before XAU finally picks a direction again.

I am still bullish on gold over the long run. Central banks—especially China—still seem far from where they ultimately want their gold allocations to be. China holds less than 8% of reserves in gold, compared with a global average of closer to 20%, which suggests to me that there is more room for continued central bank buying.

Q3'2025 Results

IAMGOLD recently reported Q3'25 Earnings in the first week of November.

Revenues rose 61% in Q3 to $707 million—thanks mostly to higher gold prices as well as Côté ramping up.

It was a strong quarter for production, with IAG putting out 190k ounces of gold, taking the year-to-date total to 524k ounces. Costs did rise a bit faster than investors would like, something management acknowledged, noting:

we understand the importance of implementing diligent cost controls particularly ahead of the next stage of organic growth.

Management also announced that IAG would be initiating a share buyback program for up to 10% of outstanding common shares. This move to return value to shareholders during a period of strong gold prices mirrors similar actions taken by other gold miners recently. While ideally, you would want companies to be buying back shares when they are most undervalued, I remain cautiously optimistic that the rally in IAG still has legs if gold approaches my target price of 5k by the end of next year. However, looking ahead, I would personally prefer a greater emphasis on returning cash via dividends, as this allows investors the flexibility to decide how best to deploy their capital—but that is merely my own preference.

Operations

Côté Gold (Ontario, Canada)

Côté (which poured first gold late in March last year) had a record Q3'25, Producing 75k gold ounces attributable to IAG, up 83% year-on-year, bringing year-to-date production to 193k ounces and keeping it on track to hit annual guidance of 250k ounces-280k ounces on an attributable basis. Mining and mill throughput were strong, with recoveries reaching a record 94% following the plant hitting full nameplate capacity in June.

Even with strong production, the quarter saw Côté’s first full maintenance shutdown, during which key equipment—like the high-pressure grinding roll and ball mill—was serviced, and the primary crusher upgraded. To keep operations running smoothly, a temporary crusher was brought in, while a secondary crusher is on track for commissioning in Q4. Once online, I expect that the additional crusher will increase capacity and help reduce maintenance-related downtime going forward.

AISC for the nine months to September came in at an impressive $1,613 per ounce sold—making Côté the lowest-cost producer among all of IAG's assets.

The Gosselin zone (which is in close proximity to Côté) looks to be shaping up as an important growth area for IAG. Drilling efforts this year have focused on confirming the size and quality of this resource and upgrading a portion of lower-confidence mineral estimates into higher-confidence categories. The program was initially planned for 45k metres of drilling, but has already expanded—with 18.5k metres drilling taking place in the third quarter, and 50k metres having taken place year-to-date. Anther 6.5k metres of drilling is planned to explore promising areas nearby—with those results expected to feed into an updated resource report by the middle of next year, which will give investors valuable visibility.

Westwood Complex (Quebec, Canada)

Westwood's gold production hit a rough patch in Q3'25. Output came in at 23k gold ounces—a 28% drop from the same period last year—bringing year-to-date output to 76k ounces. At this pace, full-year production for this asset looks set to come in below IAG's guidance range of 125koz to 140koz (with management admitting in Q3 earnings that they are unlikely to reach even the lower end of that guidance range).

Mining activity, however, was anything but slow in Q3. The crews moved 405k tonnes of ore in the quarter, up a whopping 88% from last year, driven largely by a very busy Grand Duc open pit. Underground mining, which minded 90k tonnes of ore, came out at 6.42 g/t—but here is where the challenges kicked in. A change in the mining sequence, tougher than expected ground conditions, and a bit more dilution than planned meant lower grades than the company had hoped. The good news to investors is that the IAG management team seems to be on top of it as they have been tweaking blasting techniques and refining stope designs. These actions have already started paying off. Underground ore in October average over 9 g/t.

Management remarked in Q3 Earnings that:

Underground mining rates are planned at 1,000 tpd from multiple active mining zones, while grade is expected to increase in the fourth quarter of 2025 as the mining activities sequence through stopes in higher grade zones and implement improved blasting controls. Open pit activities from Grand Duc are currently being evaluated for an expansion and extension of the pit, given the elevated gold price, with a decision to be made in the fourth quarter of 2025.

AISC for Q3 were severely impacted by all of this, and came in at $2,793 per ounce.

Essakane (Burkina Faso)

Essakane's gold production hit 92k ounces in the third quarter, down 8% from the same period last year—and brings year-to-date production to 255k ounces. IAMGOLD's stake in Essakane dropped from 90% to 85%, after Burkina Faso's government "took" an extra 5% stake—courtesy of the new mining code rolled out in August 2024 under the beret-wearing leadership of Ibrahim Traoré.

Full-year attributable production for Essakane is therefore expected to land at the low end of the guided 360k-400k ounces range.

Earlier the year in April, the Burkina Faso government also increase gold royalties. Previously, gold sold at or above $2,000/oz carried a 7% royalty. Under the new rules, gold priced at $3,000/oz or above is now taxed at 8%, with the rate rising by 1% for each additional $500/oz increment.

It definitely hasn't been an easy quarter for Essakane. Management also commented in Q3 Earnings on the security situation:

Security-related incidents are still occurring in the country, and more broadly, the West African region. The situation in Burkina Faso continues to pressure supply chains, and although the impact has lessened, in-country fuel shortages necessitated a temporary reduction in mining activity in the third quarter. The processing plant remained fully operational during this period. The Company continues to take proactive measures to ensure the safety and security of in-country personnel and is constantly adjusting its protocols and activity levels at the site in response to the security environment.

Exploration

In October, IAMGOLD made a bold move and announced the acquisition of Northern Superior and Orbec, consolidating a massive 134k hectare land package in the Chibougamau district. I like this move—it gives IAG one of Canada's largest pre-production gold camps and creates a real "hub" opportunity, where multiple deposits could feed a central processing facility. I think the numbers also back it up. The combined Nelligan Mining Complex now boasts 3.75Moz Measured and Indicated, and 8.65Moz Inferred resources. Nelligan itself continues to impress me. Indicated ounces jumped 56% to 3.1Moz, and Inferred ounces rose 33% to 5.2Moz this year, with some juicy drill hits also catching my attention. Monster Lake, just 15km North, also looks to be a high-grade gem—with drill results showing that the veins are consistent and strong.

In my view, IAMGOLD is building something here that could pay off over the long term—and I especially like the fact that they are doing so in the Tier 1 mining jurisdiction of Canada. The combination of scale, high-grade zones, and a potential central mill is exactly the kind of setup that gives optionality and flexibility, in my opinion.

Risks

As is clear to anyone reading this article, IAMGOLD is dealing with its fair share of challenges. Essakane is navigating security risks and fuel shortages, as well as clear jurisdictional risks. Westwood's underground operations have shown how tough underground mining can be at times. On top of that, I'm a bit weary about near-term gold prices after the strong rally over the last couple of months. Mining is never straightforward, and while I remain bullish on gold over the long term, investors should expect some bumps along the way.

Valuation

I have selected the below peers for a relative valuation comparison with IAMGOLD. I focused on mid-tier gold producers and development-stage miners with similar operational footprints, cost structures, and growth profiles.

I prefer using FWD EV/EBITDA ratios when valuing mining companies, as this tells me what I am paying for the underlying operations relative to the earnings they are forecast to generate. Over the past two years, IAMGOLD traded at a FWD EV/EBITDA ratio that was 14% below peers—and that is exactly where it is still trading at, suggesting to me that shares are fairly valued at current levels.

Interestingly, IAG (in the orange line below) has significantly outperformed almost all of the peers listed above—except New Gold, which is one of my favourite miners in the space.

Analyst Recommendations

A majority of analysts that publish on the Bloomberg Terminal have a "Buy" rating for IAG—and a consensus 12-month price target that is 19.4% above the current share price.

The Technical Take

Following the recent pullback towards the 50-day moving average, shares bounced off that level and are exhibiting strong upward momentum again. The MACD indicator is also in bullish territory. That said, I would like to see trading volumes pick up again before I can be confident that the rally has genuine strength.

Conclusion

I am upgrading IAMGOLD from Hold to Buy, and agree with the 12-month consensus price target of $16.39. This isn't necessarily because the company is materially better than peers—its quality remains below many peers in my opinion—but because I am taking a slightly more constructive approach with my ratings, evaluating the stock within the broader investable universe rather than limiting the comparison to its sector peers. Marginal, lower-quality producers like IAG can outperform quality miners when gold rallies sharply, as we have seen recently. The technicals also look enticing here—and a Hold rating may simply be too conservative. However, my Buy rating is contingent on the gold rally continuing, as IAG will likely underperform other higher quality names like AngloGold, when gold prices retreat sharply.

data.title: The Outlook For S&P 500 Dividends In November 2025
data.description:
The S&P 500's dividend outlook for the final quarter of 2025 brightened in the month since our last snapshot of their outlook.

On 15 October 2025, the CME Group's S&P 500 Quarterly Dividend Index' Futures quotes indicated investors could expect to collect $19.90 per share during the period covered by 2025-Q4 dividend futures contracts. On Friday, 14 November 2025, that rose to $19.94 per share, an increase of 0.2% from the previous month's outlook.

Our October 2025 snapshot also presented the projections for the S&P 500's dividends per share through the second quarter of 2026. Here is how the dividends expected in the future quarters of 2026-Q1 and 2026-Q2 changed in the past month:

  • 2026-Q1: Decrease of $0.01, dipping to $20.89 per share
  • 2026-Q2: Increase of $0.02, rising to $19.73 per share

For this dividend snapshot, we're extending the outlook for the S&P 500's expected dividends per share through the fourth quarter of 2026. The following chart presents the S&P 500's quarterly dividends per share as provided by dividend futures contracts from 2024-Q1 through 2026-Q4.

A note of caution on the dividend projection for 2026-Q4. Because it covers expectations that are currently more than four quarters into the future, it's best understood as an initial projection based on very thin trading at this point of time. The projection for this quarter can be expected to change once it falls within the typical year-ahead period that defines the typical time horizon for investors and the investment activity for its related dividend futures contract picks up.

More About Dividend Futures Data

How changes in the outlook for dividends at specific points of time in the future contribute to changes in stock prices is described by this math.

For this series, we have been taking a snapshot of the CME Group's S&P 500 quarterly dividend futures data shortly after the second or third week of each month.

Dividend futures indicate the amount of dividends per share to be paid out over the period covered by each quarter's dividend futures contracts, which start on the day after the preceding quarter's dividend futures contracts expire and end on the third Friday of the month ending the indicated quarter. As determined by dividend futures contracts, the now "current" quarter of 2025-Q4 began on Saturday, 20 September 2025, and will end on Friday, 19 December 2025. From the perspective of dividend futures, the next quarter of 2026-Q1 will begin on Monday, 22 December 2025.

Because dividend futures are tied to options contracts that run on this schedule, that makes these figures different from the quarterly dividends per share figures that are reported by Standard & Poor's. S&P reports the amount of dividends per share paid out during regular calendar quarters after the end of each quarter. This term mismatch accounts for the differences in dividends reported by both sources, with the biggest differences between the two typically seen in the first and fourth quarters of each year.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Sweetgreen: Can't Be Saved By Millennials And Gen Z
data.description:
Sweetgreen has had a rough 2025 as the company has continued to see traffic decline which has led to a decrease in the company’s revenue. I noted several challenges in my prior article, and many of these headwinds remain ongoing.

Year to date Sweetgreen is now down over 80% which is nowhere near the S&P 500’s annual YTD return of roughly 15%.

I’m reiterating my “Hold” rating as I do like some of the moves the company is making and from a valuation perspective the stock is now very reasonable. However, there are some internal issues as well as negative macro factors. Let’s dive in and discuss macro conditions as well as the recent quarterly results.

Gen Z and Millennials Can’t Save Sweetgreen

As noted in a University of Michigan survey, consumer sentiment is at its lowest point in over three years. Much of this consumer anxiety is due to the government shutdown, which should be nearing a resolution. Still, as stated in the article, many middle class Americans are feeling financial strain. I’d say Gen Z and Millennials are no exception to that strain. According to Smart Asset, roughly 73% of all wealth in the United States is held by Americans over 55 and beyond (the Baby Boomer generation).

Given that data, it’s no surprise to see consumers like Cava (CAVA), Chipotle (CMG), and Sweetgreen (SG) struggling as these are a few Millennial and Gen Z restaurant favorites. On the company’s Q3 earnings call, new CFO Jamie McConnell stated, “So the 25 to 35 consumer is the most under pressure, and they make up about 30% of our consumer base, and they're down about 15%.”

I will say, I like that Sweetgreen’s management team stated the company is launching a “protein” campaign where patrons can select 1 of 9 menu items with more than 30 grams of protein. Management also noted that the protein portions have increased by 25% which I think is a smart move given the current macro-environment and seeing that Millennials and Gen Z consumers have frequently complained about Chipotle’s inconsistent protein portions.

Despite these seemingly positive menu moves, I still worry Sweetgreen isn’t doing enough to get new customers into the door as traffic dropped again this quarter and has now been trending in an unfavorable direction as you can see below:

These menu moves seem primarily focused on the same demographic. Unless the company begins targeting different consumers or creating lower priced value items I’m not sure this move is enough to differentiate their offerings from the competition.

Operational Efficiencies

In my last article, I mentioned Sweetgreen hired Jason Cochran as their Chief Operating Officer. At the time, Cochran believed only one-third of stores were meeting his operational standards. Cochran launched an initiative he dubbed, Project One Best Way, to create standards to establish operational efficiencies. This quarter the company launched Sweetpass, which provides a framework team members can use to get up to snuff. Cochran also created “scorecards” to give to stores to give them better visibility into company metrics and easily illustrate where stores are succeeding or faltering.

After these initiatives, Sweetgreen's management believes that now 60% of stores now meet operational standards. While these are nice sounding objectives, these really mean nothing until traffic improves and these improvements in operational metrics correlate to improvements in financial metrics.

From an operational perspective, I believe it’s worth noting that earlier this month, the company decided to sell Spyce Food Co. to Wonder Group. Spyce Food is the business unit responsible for developing the company’s Infinite Kitchens. On the earnings call, here is what CEO Jonathan Neman had to say about the sale, “Under our agreement with Wonder, Sweetgreen will continue to utilize and expand Infinite Kitchen technology across our restaurants. Partnering with Wonder enables us to leverage their manufacturing scale, R&D investments and shared innovation, accelerating the refinement and rollout of additional IK units. This transaction also allows us to sharpen our focus on our core restaurant business, allocating more of our talent and financial resources toward accelerating growth and achieving profitability. The $186.4 million sale is expected to infuse our balance sheet with approximately $100 million in liquidity, strengthening our financial position and enhancing our flexibility to fund future growth initiatives.”

I think there were some really interesting questions raised by analysts on the earnings call about this sale, as a few questioned the reasoning and timing behind the decision. Personally, I don’t think Sweetgreen would be selling their Infinite Kitchens if the company was in a position of strength. If you look at the company’s cash balance over the last few quarters you can see the business has steadily been losing more cash:

To me this deal was done to provide an influx of cash as the company plans to open 15-20 net store openings in 2026.

Insider Buying

As many of my readers will know, I'm a big fan of founder-led organizations as I believe they tend to beat the broader market over time. Recently, co-founder and CEO, Jonathan Neman purchased shares as did the company's Chief Concept Officer, Jammet Nicolas. I believe there are numerous reasons why a CEO might sell but there's only one reason to buy and that's because he or she has a bullish future outlook on the business. This roughly $1 million dollar purchase by Neman illustrates his conviction in the business and his belief he can turn things around.

Financials

Sweetgreen generated revenue of roughly $172 million in Q3 2025 which was only a slight decrease compared to Q3 2024. As I noted above, same-store sales declined by 9.5%, whereas the company saw same-store sales increase by 5.6% in the prior year’s third quarter. Both restaurant-level profit and restaurant level profit margin declined compared to prior year as Q3 2025 restaurant-level profit was $22.5 million and profit margins were 13.1% In Q3 2024 restaurant-level profit was $34.9 million and profit margins were 20.1%.

Coupled with higher restaurant operating costs and higher operating expenses this has led to an increase in net loss and net loss per share for both the 2025 three month and nine month periods as you can see below:

Sweetgreen investors continue to see more dilution as the number of weighted average shares outstanding has risen over the year.

Like last quarter, management revised their guidance downward. Management had expected to have negative same-store sales growth of -4% to -6%. Now management expects declines of -7.7% to -8.5%. Revenue is expected to be in the range of $682 to $688 million, versus $700 to $715 million in the prior guide. The company is expecting restaurant level profit margin to be around 14.5% compared to 17.5% in the prior guide and adjusted EBITDA has been revised downward to $10-$13 million for the remainder of fiscal year 2025.

While much of this isn’t promising Sweetgreen does have a decent balance sheet as you can see below with still more current assets than current liabilities:

Plus the company is getting a cash influx thanks to their recent sale.

Valuation

Sweetgreen has a valuation grade of a "D+" at Seeking Alpha:

Sweetgreen continues to remain unprofitable, so I believe price to sales is likely the best valuation metric for this business.

Even though Sweetgreen's current forward P/S ratio is higher than the sector median, if you compare the company's P/S ratio to other high-growth restaurants such as Dutch Bros (BROS) Chipotle (CMG), and CAVA (CAVA), Sweetgreen is the least expensive of the four and outside of CAVA I'd say their P/S ratio has seen the second biggest decline:

Analysts still expect Sweetgreen to deliver single to double-digit revenue growth over the next 5 years and Wall Street remains bullish on the company as the average target stock price is $8.23, implying strong upside as you can see below:

Conclusion

Outside of increases to protein sizes, there isn’t much to like about this quarter. The company has continued to see sales and traffic decline as profit margins shrink.

Sweetgreen has instituted numerous initiatives to improve operational efficiencies but I’m not sure these will prove to be worthwhile given the state of macro-economy and specifically how generations with less wealth, such as Millennials and Gen Z are facing tighter budgets.

Despite the negatives, I do love the fact Neman purchased more shares. To me this is a bullish sign. I also think Sweetgreen is less expensive compared to earlier in the year and is less expensive compared to other higher-growth restaurant companies.

However, I don’t think this will be a quick turnaround and I’d only purchase shares if I was a long term investor betting on the prospect young consumers will continue to patronize Sweetgreen in search of more health-conscious offerings.

data.title: Sticky U.K. Inflation Unlikely To Stop A December Rate Cut
data.description:
By James Smith, Developed Markets Economist, UK

UK headline inflation is undoubtedly past the peak, following a smaller household energy price hike than a year earlier. But the dip from 3.8% to 3.6% was shallower than expected on account of a sharper pick-up in food inflation than we and others had pencilled in. Last month’s fall in food from 5.1% to 4.5% in annual terms looked a bit weird, and October’s rebound to 4.9% confirms that was indeed the case.

Fortunately, evidence from the eurozone – where fresh food inflation is falling – coupled with a levelling off in the newly reinstated producer price data for food products, suggests inflation at the supermarkets is probably more or less at its peak. We suspect the risk to the Bank of England’s year-end food forecast of 5.3% is to the downside.

Food inflation picked up again in October

For now, however, the recent rebound is likely to keep concern alive among the Bank’s hawks about the potential impact on inflation expectations. They worry that this could fuel a more persistent bout of price pressure, akin to what we saw two or three years ago.

Elsewhere, services inflation notched lower from 4.7% to 4.5%, but this is principally down to airfares. Once you strip out the volatile and indexed elements, our calculation of the BoE’s preferred “core services” metric actually notched a tad higher in October. The hawks will point to restaurant/café prices, often seen as a bellwether for “persistence” among services categories, where prices increased sharply on the month, potentially linked to the broader food price pressure.

'Core services' inflation nudged slightly higher in October

That said, taking a broader view, services are still generally heading in the right direction once you ignore the month-to-month volatility. Remember too that wage growth in the private sector is easing rapidly. We doubt there’s anything that will shake the conviction of the Bank’s doves, who argue immediate easing is necessary to offset potential weakness emanating from the jobs market.

The bottom line here is that there’s unlikely to be anything in this data that will change the minds of many voters on the BoE committee. That means the decision continues to hinge on Governor Andrew Bailey, who is somewhere between the two camps – though given he is more sympathetic to the view of the doves, we think he will still tip the balance in favour of a cut in December.

We doubt the forthcoming Autumn Budget will change that either. While gilt yields have risen following reports that the government has scrapped plans to increase income tax on account of a smaller fiscal hole, we are still probably looking at material tax hikes in 2026. At the margin, that helps build the case for a further loosening in monetary policy.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Flowers Foods: Dividend Cut More Likely After Q3'25 Results
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Flowers Foods Inc. (FLO) is the second-largest bakery in the US, with popular bread brands such as Dave's Killer Bread and Nature's Own.

A 45% share price decline this year pushed dividend yields to ~8% and the P/E ratio down to 11x.

This is an unusual situation.

Consumer staples are defensive stocks. Their income, and in the case of FLO, the stock price, is relatively stable, as evidenced by its 0.29 beta - a measure of correlation between FLO's share price and the market index. A low beta is a positive trait, implying low volatility during down cycles.

The main question now is, how safe is the dividend, and why are shares in free fall? Shares are down 45% this year, and dividend yield is the highest in decades.

There are multiple reasons:

  1. People are eating less bread than before.
  2. The market is pricing in a dividend cut.
  3. The investor base is shifting.

On the surface, a contrarian trade might seem a compelling play. There is a chance that the company may not cut the dividend. The market is trading as if it already has, or the prospect of a dividend cut is absolutely certain, which is not the case.

On the other hand, management hasn't ruled out a dividend cut. Normally, when there is a chance of a rebound, executives are typically bullish and reassure shareholders that dividends are safe. In recent quarters, when FLO's executives were asked about this, they said that the priority is maintaining an investment-grade rating and reducing debt. This is a case where investors should put more value on what's not being said, as opposed to what is.

Revenue Trends

People are eating less bread. FLO's executive team said as much. Making things worse is the persistence of these trends, which have been attributed to a set of factors, including what management describes as a "generational shift" in eating habits, and possibly the rise of weight loss drugs.

Organic sales growth was down 3% this year (40-week period ended October 4, 2025). The company is cutting prices in an attempt to boost volumes, but this is barely working. This year, they cut prices by 1.2%, but volumes were still down by 1.9%. In Q3'25, they doubled down on this strategy, making a steeper 2.3% discount, and this slowed the volume decline a bit, to 0.6%. This doesn't show in the consolidated GAAP earnings and revenue, because FLO bought Simple Mills, which boosted sales by 5% in Q3'25. Excluding this acquisition, sales were down 2.9% in Q3'25, and 3% in the three fiscal quarters ended October 4, 2025.

Consensus Wall Street estimates point to a 3.3% sales growth for FY2025, which implies a continuation of the current trends - volume and prices declines offset by the Simple Mills acquisition. Management recently lowered FY25 guidance to $5.02 to $5.08 billion in sales without the Simple Mills contribution, suggesting between -0.4% to -1.6% YoY organic revenue decline compared to FY2024.

It is worth noting that Q4'25 will have an extra week more than Q4'24.

Is Dividend Safe?

FLO has been growing its annual distributions for 22 years (notwithstanding Q2'2013, when a stock split adjusted the real dividend lower temporarily).

This historical commitment to shareholders contradicts what management is saying (or more accurately, not saying).

When asked about the narrow gap between earnings and dividend payout, the company's CFO failed to reassure investors, stating:

with regard to kind of the capital allocation structure. I mean, we've always taken -- tried to take a balanced approach. And you you're right, the board evaluates this quarter-to-quarter and they'll take into consideration performance, cash flow, liquidity, and this will be no different. We'll continue to have those conversations and we'll continue to make decisions as things progress - FLO Q2'25 Earnings call.

Lower sales, contracting margins, and increasing debt costs on the back of the recent acquisitions pushed the payout ratio above 100% in Q3'25. If these trends continue, management might be forced to prioritize balance sheet stability over distributions.

Max Gumport from BNP Paribas noted that the company's decision to lower CapEx seems like an "early admission" that the dividend isn't sustainable. Again, management failed to challenge this narrative, restating that the board reassesses dividends on a quarterly basis.

The strongest signal around a potential dividend cut came in Q3'25, when management stated:

Our capital allocation priority is to maintain an investment-grade rating, while returning to a more normalized leverage ratio, enabling us to explore further growth investments. FLO Q3'25 Earnings Call
9 months ended Sept Net income (m $) Dividends paid (m $) Payout Ratio
2025 $ 150.90 $ 157.00 104%
2024 $ 205.00 $ 152.50 74%

On a cash basis, the company still generates more cash than it spends on dividends. CapEx has generally hovered below depreciation expense, but not always. Roughly a dozen of the company's bakeries have an Energy Star certification from the EPA, a testament to quality and, more importantly, competitiveness compare to other US-based bakeries. However, that leaves dozens of other bakeries without a certification, which suggests a potential need for replacing the industrial ovens. As of 2024, FLO operated 45 bakeries (p.24).

Generally, cash dividend coverage ratios are an unsustainable measure of dividend stability, although it does assuage the urgency to cut dividends, giving management more time to balance its accounts. Seeking Alpha's Quant Score accurately captures these dynamics, with a C+ dividend safety rating.

Valuation

FLO is undervalued compared to its historical averages, but the undervaluation hypothesis dims ever slightly when compared to peers. At a forward EV/EBITDA of 8.6x, it's at the lower bound of peers, but still within industry range, despite its dramatic share price drop this year.

For the valuation multiples to rebound, or at least rise above the industry median average, investors will need to see signs of stabilization in revenue and earnings, and more clarity over dividends going forward. Seeking Alpha captures these dynamics accurately, with a "Sell" overall rating despite an A score on Valuation.

Final Thoughts and Risks

FLO's share price decline this year doesn't only mirror investors' disappointment over performance and the dividend risk, but it also highlights the risk-averse nature of the company's shareholder base, who naturally fled seeing the once-stable company struggle to maintain sales (notwithstanding the Simple Mills acquisition).

For some, FLO might appear a contrarian play. If things stabilize and the company maintains its dividend, we could see a rebound in the stock price, complemented by an 8% dividend yield. But management's commentary, or lack thereof, suggests that they will cut distributions.

There are risks to this sell hypothesis. First, the share price seems to have digested a lot of pessimism, and shares might actually go up if FLO doesn't cut the dividend, or if the dividend cut is smaller than what is reflected in the share price. The company is leaning into new verticals, chasing a bifurcated demand between high-end and value products. Snacks and healthy "better-for-you" categories are also seeing sustained demand. For example, Dave's Killer Bread, which includes whole grain products, saw unit volumes up 10% in Q3'25. Similarly, Canyon Bakehouse, which offers gluten-free bakeries, saw unit volumes up 6% during the same period.

The demand dynamics have become complex, but what is clear is that the traditional bread category, where FLO has a large exposure, is suffering the most.

data.title: Ares Management: Buying Opportunity On Pullback
data.description:
Introduction

When I wrote about Ares (NYSE:ARES) back in April I saw an opportunity to buy into a leading name in the private credit space following a sharp sell off in the stock. Since my initial recommendation the stock has delivered a total return of just shy of 10%. Unfortunately if I had written this article in mid-August I would be saying my stock pick had risen by closer to 40%.

So in today's article I will unpack what has happened with the company and the industry since my prior coverage. In my view this stock volatility is a function of a few idiosyncratic happenings in the last few weeks. Ares Q3 earnings looked pretty good to me and I will discuss them in detail below. I issued a buy rating on the stock in April and I am happy to reiterate that rating today as I believe the core business continues to perform nicely.

Recent fears about the risks inherent in private credit should not be dismissed out of hand. However, investors should also balance those potential risks with the fact that Ares is a top tier player in a section of the financial world that has seen tremendous growth and is expected to continue doing so. Even when we take account of the very sharp pull back in shares since mid-August, an investor would have still made close to a 100% return over the past 3 years. A figure well in excess of the market return.

Credit Cockroaches

The aggressive sell off in Ares I believe is a direct result of a couple of high profile bankruptcy cases in the US and their aftermath. On the 24th of September a company called First Brands filed for chapter 11, the firm had borrowed heavily from so-called non-depository financial institutions or NDFIs. This category includes private markets groups such as Ares and its peers. The first Brands event quickly followed another bankruptcy of a firm called Tricolor earlier in the month.

As if two quick fire bankruptcies wasn't enough, the market got really spooked when JPMorgan (NYSE:JPM) CEO Jamie Dimon noted on their Q3 earnings that when you see one cockroach there are likely others. The knee-jerk reaction from markets was to fear a potential systemic private credit cascade. The lack of transparency of private markets, no doubt reminds investors of the opaque nature of synthetic derivatives used in the lead up to the 2008 financial crisis. Similar to that time, investor worry that hidden from view, some private markets players could have amassed a huge amount of risk which if unraveled could cause a system wide event.

This is by no means a trivial thing to worry about and it makes the pullback in the private markets names somewhat understandable, investors are trying to quantify what exactly the risk profile of these companies are. So why am I not overly concerned right now.

For a start, these loans were actually bank-led loans rather than NDFI loans. Big banks such as JPMorgan and Bank of America (NYSE:BAC) reported provisions in their Q3 earnings related to Tricolor and First Brands. So if we want to point the finger about lending standards, maybe the banks are a better place to start. Blackstone (NYSE:BX) CEO Stephen Schwarzman summed it up nicely on their earnings release

“Despite all these positive developments, over the past several weeks, there's been a significant external focus on the implications of certain credit defaults in the market. These events have been erroneously linked to the traditional private credit market as a result of misunderstandings and misinformation.

Importantly, the defaults and focus resulted from bank-led and bank syndicated credits, not private credit. Moreover, these situations are widely believed to involve the fraudulent pledging of the same collateral to multiple parties.

The traditional private credit model is characterized by direct origination in the context of a long-term hold strategy, with due diligence performed by sophisticated institutional managers and rigorously negotiated documentation.” (Stephen Schwarzman, Q3 Earnings)

However, I don't think pointing a finger at banks is a worthwhile exercise here either. The first reason is simply the fact that when lending you naturally expect some small percentage of loans will run into distress and have to be written off. That's simply the way it is with lending, always has been and will be. JPMorgan for instance reported credit costs in Q3 which were broadly in-line with the prior year, hardly indicative of a credit crisis.

On the chart below the impact of the bankruptcies has a clear impact on Ares. The arrow indicates the 24th of September with the filing of First Brands Bankruptcy. The stock fell precipitously on the day and has yet to recover.

Earnings

With all the noise heading into earnings there was an onus on Ares to assuage fears with a decent set of numbers and I believe they achieved that. Looking into some of the key reported metrics, I think they read very nicely. Fee Related earnings grew by 39% with the core basic management fees on AUM growing by 28%. AUM grew to just shy of $600bn on a really good quarter of fundraising for the firm. New funds in the door were a touch below $31bn which is a big number for a firm of this size, nearly 5% of total AUM as inflows in just a single quarter, a figure that speaks to a robust appetite for exposure to private credit.

Around 2/3rds of the funds in were for private credit funds but the remaining third saw a balanced allocation between real asset funds and secondaries, so while credit is the big draw, its not the only game in town for Ares. This fundraising in the quarter is broadly in line with the overall group AUM, naturally we see concentration in credit but a spread across other asset classes for roughly one third of the overall funds.

Ares has around $150bn of capital available for deployment or so-called dry powder. While this level of capital is indeed an asset and allows the firm to be opportunistic when it sees opportunities, it also plays into one of the market's fears. I get the sense that investors are concerned that too much money has flowed into these private capital groups.

Ares cannot earn money unless it puts that capital to work by investing it in deals. That pressure to try put investor capital to work creates the fear that shortcuts will be taken in search of deals. Ares has always fallen back on its long-term track record of success as an indicator of the quality of its processes. Bears would argue Ares and its peers have never had to contend with this level of capital before, the temptation to get deals done could prove too great to resist in search of returns.

While concerns will abound in the near term, I believe Ares earnings show no sign of let up. The firm is minting management fees from its rapidly growing AUM. The sell off in the stock has certainly taken any potential froth out of the stock. Over the long term this company is a proven compounder and I think investors shouldn't read too much into the recent idiosyncratic bankruptcies that captured the news headlines. As I said earlier, the nature of lending is that some companies will go bankrupt and not be able to repay, its a feature of the business not bug, and certainly not a cockroach.

Risks

The risks here are obvious I think and should be clear from what I've written above. The big risk is my thesis is wrong, the recent bankruptcies are the first indications of major issues in the credit world which will cascade and reveal loan books in private markets that are stuffed with junk loans that will be unable to repay as soon as the economy weakens to any meaningful degree. I do not believe this to be the case but if an investor is looking at Ares and thinking what is the big risk here I think a credit event has to be the thing to be watchful of.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my buy thesis on Ares back in April has played out ok I would say. The stock has delivered 10% return which isn't bad but recent months have been challenging in terms of price performance. However, the underlying business has continued to grow nicely and Q3 earnings displayed really robust numbers on the fee growth. I am of the opinion that recent stresses in the credit market are nothing more than run of the mill bankruptcies that happened to be amplified by comments made by Jamie Dimon. I reiterate my buy position on Ares as private credit is a big growth area of finance and Ares has a proven track record.

data.title: Amazon Stock (AMZN): Strong Buy Or Not? | 2 Minute Analysis
data.description:

Jumping over to the Growth grade, which is currently A-, while the revenue growth year-over-year is 10.87, compared to the sector median of just 2.82, but also the EPS GAAP growth is almost 91% year-over-year, compared to the sector at just 1.5% showcasing robust financial performance.

Jumping into the Profitability grade, which is currently A+, while net income margin is almost 11%, compared to the sector at 4.34%, which is nice to see, but I also want to highlight this. Cash from operations is a 121.14 billion, compared to the sector at just 285 million. Talk about a cash cow here.

Jumping into the Momentum, B- grade here, and that's due to the likes of one-year price performance being 22.6%, compared to the sector at negative 2, but more recently on the three month, we have negative 0.76, compared to the sector, which is up 2.39. So, it's showing a little bit of consolidation in the near term is what it looks like.

And then lastly, for Revisions, B+ grade on this one, that's due to the earnings per share having 50 up revisions, but it does have one down revision in the last three months. And if we look at the revenue numbers, that is 52 up revisions and one down revision in the last month. So that one down revision weighing on the grade slightly there.

So, is now the time to add the stock to your portfolio? Well, if we look at some of the recent analysis written by Seeking Alpha Analysts, it looks like Buy is pretty much across the board. data.title: Nike Stock (NKE): Is It A Buy, Hold, Or Sell? | 2-Minute Analysis

Get Started With Seeking Alpha Premium Today!

This video's transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and information purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

Daniel Snyder: Welcome to 2 Minute Analysis. I'm Daniel Snyder from Seeking Alpha. In this series, we dive into the stocks that you request. Our goal is to not only entertain, but provide value and insights about the investments you care about. So, let's throw 2 minutes on the clock and dive-in.

Today, we're diving into Nike Incorporated, ticker symbol, NKE. Let's kick things off here with looking at the Seeking Alpha Quant system, which currently has a Hold rating on the stock. Jumping over to the Momentum grade, which is currently C, that is due to the one-year price performance of being down negative 22.5%. However, in the last three months, Nike stock is up 10.83% showing some of that positive investor activity more recently.

data.title: Top Income And AI Growth Stocks Worth Watching
data.description:
Cautious Fed Spurring Market Rotation

The start to Q4 has been a battle of the bulls and the bears, as renewed tariff concerns and a government shutdown reignited volatility despite solid Q3 earnings. The shutdown, which began on Oct. 1 and lasted through last week, sparked a tech stock selloff and highlighted investor anxiety over stretched valuations. Although the shutdown's end brought a brief moment of optimism, index performance was mixed, and tech stocks finished last week flat.

Tech performance was subdued despite an end to the government shutdown.

One secondary consequence of the shutdown has been the gap in economic reporting, fueling debate over the likelihood of a final rate cut for the year. Without official government data on jobs or inflation, investors have turned to private sources, which in turn have amplified market speculation. Industry experts and Fed officials have recently cast doubt on the necessity of a third rate cut this year, which has contributed to negative sentiment. The FedSpeak Index tracks key messages from Federal Reserve communications, helping investors interpret the monetary policy outlook, and remarks have grown more hawkish recently, signaling that a rate cut may not be forthcoming

Source Link: Bloomberg

In the coming weeks, the backlog of economic data will provide investors with plenty to digest before the Federal Reserve’s December meeting. Expectations center on several key releases, including the September Jobs Report on Nov. 20. This data deluge could fuel another wave of volatility as markets recalibrate around underlying economic trends. The biggest debate centers on the labor market; if job growth remains robust, the case for a rate cut weakens, potentially sparking renewed volatility. Conversely, weaker-than-expected labor data would solidify the case for a rate cut, potentially reversing sentiment.

Nvidia in Focus: Jitters Over Debt-Fueled AI Bets

Investor anticipation is running high ahead of Nvidia's earnings release, which is often seen as a bellwether for the AI sector. With ongoing headlines around major strategic investments in AI infrastructure, analysts will be carefully watching for evidence that these investments are fueling revenue growth and sustaining margin strength. A shift is underway in the once ironclad balance sheets of tech giants as these companies use debt to finance their AI infrastructure spending. With capital expenditures reaching new heights, the prospect of a rate cut is even more critical to support ongoing investment and manage higher financing costs in the AI sector.

Top Income and AI Growth Stocks Worth Watching

Given that the market appears to be at an inflection point, SA Quant analyzed both sides of potential Fed policy moves. If weak labor suggests a rate cut, this could provide fresh tailwinds for AI stocks, whose valuations have likely improved since the pullback. Conversely, signs that the Fed may hold rates steady could favor dividend stocks, as income-oriented investors tend to seek stable cash flows and reliable balance sheets amid market volatility.

The AI stocks highlighted below represent quality names in the space; I steadily avoided the “cockroaches” with poor fundamentals that I discussed in my recent 5 Tech and Consumer Stocks at Risk of Steep Declines. One of these AI stocks has been the top performer from my Top Quant Stocks Of 2025 list, gaining nearly 200% since its selection on Jan. 9, 2025, while another hails from my Top Stocks for H2 2025 list. All three of the stocks below are current members of the Alpha Picks portfolio that delivers two best-of-the-best stocks on a monthly basis by email—its performance since inception is incredible, +254% compared to the S&P 500’s 78% (as of 11/17/25).

Top AI Stocks Worth Watching possess exceptional fundamentals, particularly in terms of growth and profitability.

On the dividend side, I focused the selection on yield strength, representing a blend of traditional stocks and high-yielding REITs.

Top Income Stocks Worth Watching showcase an average forward yield of 5.84% vs. 1.09% for the S&P 500 and 1.59% for the VIG.

These three stocks boast an average forward dividend yield of 5.84%, backed by exceptional dividend grades. Below, I will examine each of the six stocks in greater detail.

Income Stocks

In the hunt for Black Friday deals, income stocks offer the kind of steady returns, reliable dividends, and inflation protection that never go out of style. Here are three top dividend plays that could help stretch your purchasing power long after the sales end.

1. OneMain Holdings (OMF)

  • Market Capitalization: $6.97B

  • Sector: Financials

  • Industry: Consumer Finance

  • FWD Yield: 7.10%

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/17/2025): 23 out of 683

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/17/2025): 2 out of 38

OneMain Holdings is a provider of personal loans and credit products to nonprime consumers in the United States and is the No. 2 Quant Rated Consumer Finance stock. The company has recently rewarded shareholders through a new $1 billion share repurchase program and a recently increased dividend.

Source: OMF Q3 2025 Investor Presentation

OMF delivered a solid double earnings beat in Q3, driven by disciplined credit management, improved delinquency, and net charge-off trends, as well as robust origination growth. These positive indicators are reflected in the company’s excellent profitability grade, supported by a 21% ROE and $3.02B in cash from operations. In terms of its growth outlook, the company offers enticing short- and long-term EPS growth, which are 42% and 94% above the sector median, respectively.

OMF Profitability Grade

Analysts have taken note of the company’s recent accomplishments, with 15 raising their estimates in the past 90 days and none revising them downward. With an FWD yield of 7.10%, dovetailed by sterling dividend safety and growth grades, OMF is well suited for high-yield investors looking for further growth potential.

2. Alpine Income Property Trust, Inc. (PINE)

  • Market Capitalization: $252.83M

  • Sector: Real Estate

  • Industry: Diversified REITs

  • FWD Yield: 6.94%

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/17/2025): 11 out of 173

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/17/2025): 1 out of 12

PINE is the No. 1 Quant-ranked Diversified REIT focused on owning and operating a portfolio of 128 commercial properties across 34 states.

Source Link: PINE Q3 2025 Investor Presentation

The company’s strategy centers on acquiring high-yielding properties, recycling capital through active property sales, and maintaining near 100% occupancy. The company also drives revenue through its portfolio of structured loans. PINE possesses excellent growth and solid profitability for the firm, highlighted by its 11% 5Y CAGR AFFO growth and 48% AFFO margin.

The company is particularly notable from a valuation and income perspective. Its FWD P/AFFO trades at a 41% discount to the sector, while offering an average four-year dividend yield that's 47% above the sector. PINE’s combination of high-quality properties, solid AFFO growth, and discounted valuation offers attractive income and upside potential with a healthy margin of safety.

3. Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK)

  • Market Capitalization: $230.63B

  • Sector: Healthcare

  • FWD Yield: 3.49%

  • Industry: Pharmaceuticals

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/17/2025): 13 out of 976

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/17/2025): 5 out of 177

This global pharmaceutical titan possesses a diverse portfolio of medicines and vaccines, accompanied by a robust R&D pipeline that includes about 80 Phase II and III trials combined. The company’s growth has been supported by its blockbuster oncology drug, KEYTRUDA, whose third-quarter sales increased 8% Y/Y. MRK showcases a roster of exceptional underlying profitability metrics, including a trailing 12-month EBITDA margin that's above the sector median and an impressive $7.30 in cash per share.

MRK Profitability Grade

MRK has benefited from the recent biotech bull market, outperforming the S&P 500 and healthcare sector (XLV) over the last month. The company also boasts a straight-A dividend report card with highlights including 14 consecutive years of dividend payments.

MRK Dividend Grades

Trading at a 40% discount to the healthcare sector in terms of its forward PEG, MRK is a high-quality, diversified addition to any dividend portfolio.

AI Stocks

Black Friday bargains could be brewing for AI‑oriented tech stocks that have recently pulled back. After months of strong momentum, the latest dip might offer investors a shot at picking up leading growth names at more attractive prices.

4. Micron Technology, Inc. (MU)

  • Market Capitalization: $277.06B

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Semiconductors

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/17/2025): 1 out of 539

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/17/2025): 1 out of 67

Micron stands out as the only U.S.-based manufacturer of advanced memory chips vital to cutting-edge technologies, including AI systems and high-performance computing. The stock has delivered a 108% return since it was first added to the Pro Quant Portfolio on Aug. 8, while Alpha Picks members have seen a big return in just over a month. As the No. 1 Quant-ranked semiconductor stock, the stock’s deeply discounted valuation is notable in a sector that has delivered relentless gains over the past 18 months. It's currently trading at an 87% discount in terms of its FWD PEG, with a price cash flow of 9.9x.

MU Valuation Grade

The stock boasts equally enviable growth and profitability characteristics. MU’s EPS growth is attractive in both the short and long term, with an FWD EPS diluted growth and 3-5Y CAGR EPS FWD long-term growth that are more than 1,000% and 329% above the sector median, respectively. Analysts are equally enthused about Micron’s prospects; the stock has received a whopping 29 FY1 revisions in the last 90 days vs. zero downward, a resounding endorsement for this domestic AI darling.

5. CommScope Holding Company, Inc. (COMM)

  • Market Capitalization: $3.72B

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Communications Equipment

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/17/2025): 2 out of 539

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/17/2025): 1 out of 39

CommScope is a global provider of connectivity solutions for data center, enterprise, and telecommunication networks. It was featured in my Top 10 Stocks for H2 2025 and has since returned 194%. Its August sale of the Connectivity and Cable Solutions segment to Amphenol for $10.5 billion was completed in a strategic move to address high leverage and refocus resources on its higher-growth segments. This move is likely to strengthen the company’s already solid profitability, which has improved from a ‘C+’ six months ago to its current ‘A’ grade. Notably, COMM possesses a Levered Free cash flow margin and a net income margin that are 140% and 275% above the sector median, respectively.

COMM Profitability Grade

COMM has delivered a remarkable run over the last year, returning just about 300%, while maintaining a highly attractive valuation. The company’s valuation report card is a sea of green, with highlights including an FWD PEG below 1x, indicating the stock is undervalued relative to its earnings growth.

COMM Valuation Grade

Positive analyst sentiment serves as yet another endorsement of the company's future potential, with abundant opportunities for growth likely to emerge following the CCS sale.

6. Celestica Inc. (CLS)

  • Market Capitalization: $35.68B

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Electronic Manufacturing Services

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 11/17/2025): 4 out of 539

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 11/17/2025): 1 out of 18

Celestica stands out as a crown jewel among AI stocks, currently holding the top spot as the highest-ranked Quant stock in the IT sector. CLS is a two-time Alpha Pick, posting an extraordinary return of over 1000% since its original selection and 289% since it was re-picked. The company specializes in design, manufacturing, and supply chain solutions, serving sectors like enterprise, aerospace, healthcare, communications, and especially data centers.

Celestica consistently surpasses expectations quarter after quarter, thanks to its critical role in AI infrastructure.

“Based on our 2025 outlook, we are on track to deliver our strongest performance on record. There are a number of key drivers supporting the sustained improvement in our performance. The first driver is capturing share in high-growth markets, with the cornerstone being our presence in AI data centers, said Robert Mionis, President, CEO and Director of CLS.

While the stock briefly tumbled last week amid the market pullback, investors may see this as an opportunity to invest in a company with extraordinary growth and profitability characteristics. Celestica showcases an EPS FWD Long-Term Growth (3-5Y CAGR) that is more than 150% above the IT sector, while offering an incredible $553M in cash from operations. The company's 13 upward analyst revisions are a testament to its fundamental strength, and despite some hesitancy in the market, CLS has a strong track record of leading the charge when investors return their focus to fundamentals.

Navigating Q4 Crosscurrents With Growth and Income

The start of Q4 has fueled heightened volatility, with renewed tariff concerns and a government shutdown dampening tech sector momentum despite solid earnings. The shutdown created gaps in economic reporting, increasing speculation over whether the Fed will proceed with another rate cut. A backlog of key economic releases is set to shape sentiment before the December Fed meeting, with job growth data pivotal to rate cut expectations and likely market volatility. Changes in Fed policy could shift the balance between fast-growing AI stocks and income-oriented dividend plays.​ Regardless of near-term market sentiment, SA Quant has identified six high-quality stocks, three focused on top income and three leading in AI innovation, that are well positioned to perform in either scenario and for the long term.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using Stock Screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Several of the stocks discussed above were initially chosen for our investment research platforms. Alpha Picks might be ideal if you're interested in two monthly stock picks of the top "strong buy" quant stocks. Seeking Alpha’s quant ratings and investment research tools help to ensure you are furnished with the best resources to make informed investment decisions while taking the emotion out of investing.

If you’re looking for more frequent investment ideas, we launched the PRO Quant Portfolio in June. It’s a weekly rebalanced selection of our Top 30 Quant Strong Buy stocks, spanning multiple regions and market caps. Happy Investing!

data.title: Credit Markets Face The AI Wave
data.description:
By Ashok Bhatia, CFA | David M. Brown, CFA | Joseph P. Lynch

As tech giants tap credit markets to finance artificial intelligence, we consider the broader impacts of AI on fixed income.

Hyperscalers Look Beyond Cash

The past few months have seen massive offerings of AI-related debt—roughly $93 billion or more than 5% of investment-grade debt issuance this year—close to triple the sector’s average annual issuance of $32 billion between 2015 and 2024, according to Bank of America. The borrowers are a "who’s who" of hyperscalers, such as Meta, Alphabet and Oracle, looking to build out data centers while seeking to secure captive energy sources to keep them running. According to Morgan Stanley, we could see an additional $3 trillion in AI-related capital expenditures over the next three years, potentially accelerating already aggressive issuance levels.

AI Data Center Borrowing Has Soared

U.S. Investment Grade Supply From Large Tech Firms ($ Billions)

For the most part, these cash-rich issuers are just beginning to take on debt to fund these needs, maintaining large capacity for new leverage without affecting credit ratings. It remains an open question whether companies and their financial backers will be rewarded for their massive spending; adoption may prove less than generally expected, or technological innovation could theoretically curb the footprint needed for effective AI delivery.

The potential safety valve—should AI disappoint—may be the ability of companies to use this computing capacity in their existing, viable businesses. That said, we think it will be important to avoid the “wrong side” of these trades, in part due to the general euphoria associated with the AI trend. Beyond the large issuers are various small companies that will occupy niche areas of the data center buildout. Some may be backstopped by the hyperscalers, but it is in this area that signs of potential trouble could emerge.

Cornucopia of Debt Sources and Structures

Given the massive scale of the spending needs, it stands to reason that these companies are employing multiple structures to bring in funding. Much of the buildout should continue to be financed from free cash flow, but beyond traditional corporate debt, issuers are also looking to asset-backed securities and various private markets. The unusual profile of data center risk—part real estate, part utility and part construction loan—lends itself to bespoke deal structures, making careful analysis essential for investors.

Where Will the Money Come From?

Can the Energy Sector Support AI Growth?

Securing energy will likely be essential to keep the AI boom going. According to estimates, U.S. data center electricity consumption could grow from today’s 175 TWh to 325 – 580 TWh by 2028.1 However, the potential avenues toward this growth will be highly constrained. It takes three to four years to build a gas turbine and, based on history, 10 years or more to construct a new nuclear plant. The big tech companies have sought to secure dedicated power sources, including recommissioned nuclear plants, but over the next few years much of development will focus on other renewable sources, which carry reliability and cost issues. Another consideration is the inflationary impact of AI-related demand on the cost of electricity to consumers, an issue that regulators will likely focus on.

Gauging Business Impacts From AI

Beyond the issuance dynamics, another closely watched aspect of AI’s rollout remains its repercussions for business models and the potential success of companies in adapting to and capitalizing on the new technology. As one would expect, much of the action in assessing credit risk is within the non-investment-grade sector. Here, we are finding that many issuers are well positioned to leverage AI advances to deliver cost efficiencies, enhance productivity and unlock potential growth drivers.

Of course, AI could also create pockets of disruption depending on an issuer’s position across the value chain. Some may need to increase investments, acquire AI capabilities and adjust operational strategies to transition their businesses.

Opportunities and Risks

The potential for disruption or opportunities generated will likely depend on an issuer’s position in the value chain and management execution

An Ongoing Analysis

Neuberger’s integrated global investment teams are actively collaborating in assessing the acceleration of AI’s development and its investment implications. We believe the hyperscalers are generally well positioned to expand their debt loads, with greater risk affecting smaller players. Dynamics across industries will vary; companies with scale, entrenched competitive positioning, strong management teams and more-advanced AI integration should be best positioned to navigate this shift. The broader impacts of AI on the economy and labor markets could be highly fluid and bear watching closely in the coming months.

1Source: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory as cited by J.P. Morgan, as of November 10, 2025.
